lesson: Phase 1 took 24 minutes — consider scope reduction
Phase 2 took 17 minutes — consider scope reduction
Phase 3 took 30 minutes — consider scope reduction
Phase 7 took 14 minutes — consider scope reduction

P0-1: Design described "lazy retirement on read" for score_decayed entries but never specified the write-back step. getGlobalLessons() would mark entries retired in memory but never persist the change, creating phantom pool occupants that block new entries forever. Root cause: designer focused on the read path (filter retired) without tracing the write path (persist retirement marks).
context: Design review v1 found getGlobalLessons() missing writeAtomic() after retirement pass. Fixed in v2 by adding explicit persist step. This is a classic "read-modify but forget to write" pattern in lazy evaluation designs.

lesson: P0-2: feedback() only searched local file but preflight injects from both local AND global. A global-only lesson would receive feedback that silently fails (ID not found in local file). Root cause: designer did not apply Caller-Side Review -- did not trace where injected lessons originate from to ensure feedback() can reach all sources.
context: Design review v1 caught this by tracing preflight injection path (local + global) against feedback search path (local only). Fixed by dual-file search in feedback(). Textbook violation of Rule 1: "not only review the producer, must review the consumer."

lesson: P1-4: Design reused the field name "mandate" for checkpoint feedback rejection, but "mandate" was already used extensively by computeNextDirective() for phase navigation. Field name collision in return objects can cause downstream consumers to misinterpret the response. Always grep for existing usages of a field name before introducing it in a new context.
context: checkpoint returns mandate from computeNextDirective() for normal flow. Adding a second mandate for lesson feedback would confuse the agent consumer. Fixed by using feedbackInstruction as the field name.

lesson: Implementation phase (Phase 3) was a one-shot pass: all 7 tasks implemented, build clean, 87/87 tests passing on first attempt. No revision needed. Key enabler: thorough design review (Phase 1 v2) that resolved all P0/P1 before coding started, plus a well-structured plan with clear task boundaries.
context: Phase 3 ran from 08:48 to 09:18 (30 minutes) for 7 tasks. Zero rework. Design iteration in Phase 1 (08:06 to 08:30, two rounds) paid off by preventing implementation-phase surprises.

lesson: Code review (Phase 4) passed with zero P0/P1 issues. All 10 ACs verified, all design review fixes confirmed in code. Only 5 P2 suggestions found. This validates that a rigorous design review + plan review pipeline produces implementation that needs minimal post-hoc correction.
context: Code review took only 4 minutes (09:18 to 09:22). The reviewer explicitly verified every P0/P1 fix from design review against actual code lines, confirming traceability from design issue to implementation fix.

lesson: Backward compatibility strategy (all new fields z.*.optional() + ensureDefaults() helper) enabled zero-migration deployment. Existing JSON files continue to work without any transformation step. This pattern is worth reusing for any schema evolution in file-persisted data.
context: types.ts uses optional Zod fields, lessons-constants.ts provides ensureDefaults() that fills score/feedbackHistory/retired. Tests #1-6 explicitly verify legacy entries get correct defaults.

lesson: Design review required exactly 1 revision cycle (v1 NEEDS_REVISION -> v2 PASS). The 2 P0 + 5 P1 issues in v1 were all substantive (data loss, silent failures, field collisions), not cosmetic. This is a healthy pattern: catching 7 blocking/important issues in design review saved significant implementation rework. Investment: ~24 min for design review (2 rounds) vs. potential multi-hour debugging if these surfaced in Phase 3/4.
context: Phase 1: 08:06 -> 08:13 (v1 review, NEEDS_REVISION) -> 08:30 (v2 review, PASS). Total 24 min. Without this, P0-1 (phantom pool) and P0-2 (silent feedback loss) would have been runtime bugs.

lesson: E2E testing (Phase 5) initially had coverage gaps for AC-1, AC-2, AC-9 (all MCP handler integration paths). 5 additional integration tests were written to fill these gaps, bringing total from 87 to 92. Lesson: MCP handler code that cannot be unit-tested in isolation must be covered by integration tests that simulate the full tool call flow. Plan should pre-allocate test tasks for handler-level ACs.
context: Phase 5 ran 09:23 to 09:30 (7 min). Code review (Phase 4) had already flagged AC-1/2/9 as coverage gaps. Phase 5 closed them with 5 integration tests (#31-35). The gap was predictable -- future plans should include handler-level test tasks.

lesson: Plan review caught 2 minor issues (readGlobalEntries extraction timing and addToGlobal visibility). Both were resolved quickly. The plan review step adds ~5 min but prevents task-ordering mistakes that can cause cascading failures during implementation.
context: Phase 2: 08:30 to 08:48 (18 min total for plan creation + review). Plan had 7 tasks covering 10 ACs. Review caught that readGlobalEntries should be extracted in Task 2 (not Task 3) and addToGlobal should stay private.

lesson: When designing "read-with-side-effects" methods (like getGlobalLessons() that reads entries but also retires decayed ones), always make the write-back explicit and unconditional at method end. Lazy mutation patterns where reads modify in-memory state are dangerous if the write-back is conditional or forgotten. Pattern: read -> mutate -> ALWAYS write back full array, even if no mutations occurred (idempotent write is safer than conditional write).
context: getGlobalLessons() applies decay, marks retirements, updates appliedCount, then writes the full entries array back. The unconditional writeAtomic at the end ensures no mutation is lost. Trade-off: slightly more I/O but eliminates the "forgot to persist" class of bugs entirely.

lesson: For dual-file write operations (local + global), use independent writeAtomic calls with .catch(() => {}) for error isolation, but return a result object indicating which writes succeeded ({ localUpdated, globalUpdated }). Silent swallowing is acceptable for resilience but the caller needs partial-success information for correct user feedback.
context: feedback() writes to local and global files independently. Design review P2-NEW-2 flagged silent error swallowing. Implementation improved on design by returning { localUpdated, globalUpdated } counts, giving callers visibility into partial failures.

lesson: When implementing "read-modify-write" on JSON files with lazy evaluation (e.g., lazy retirement in getGlobalLessons), always persist the modified state at the end of the read method. Use writeAtomic (write-to-tmp + rename) for crash safety. The "write on every read" tradeoff is acceptable for single-session file-based storage but must be revisited for multi-session concurrent access.
context: getGlobalLessons() applies decay, marks retirements, updates appliedCount, then writeAtomic() at the end. P2-NEW-3 in design review noted the write-on-read frequency concern for future multi-session scenarios. Current single-session usage is safe.

lesson: For dual-file write operations (local + global), use independent writeAtomic + .catch(() => {}) for error isolation so one file failure does not block the other. However, return structured results (e.g., { localUpdated, globalUpdated }) so callers can detect partial failures rather than silently swallowing errors.
context: feedback() writes to local and global files independently. Design review P2-NEW-2 flagged silent error swallowing. Implementation addressed this by returning update counts. Pattern applicable to any system writing to multiple storage backends.

lesson: Zod optional() fields + a centralized ensureDefaults() helper is an effective schema evolution strategy for JSON file storage. All new fields are z.*.optional() in the schema, and ensureDefaults() fills them with sensible values on read. This avoids migration scripts entirely. The key discipline: every code path that reads entries must either call ensureDefaults() or handle undefined with ?? fallback (e.g., entry.score ?? initialScore(entry.severity)).
context: types.ts defines optional fields, lessons-constants.ts provides ensureDefaults(). Code review noted that ensureDefaults() is not called at the read boundary (readGlobalEntries) but instead downstream consumers handle undefined. Both patterns work but centralizing at read boundary is cleaner.
